snippets.title: Indexing, slicing, mutation
snippets.code:
messages = ["sys", "u1", "a1", "u2"]

print(messages[0])      # 'sys'     - O(1) random access
print(messages[-1])     # 'u2'      - negative index counts from end
print(messages[1:3])    # ['u1','a1'] - slice is a NEW list

messages.append("a2")   # amortized O(1)
messages[0] = "system"   # O(1) overwrite
print(messages)         # ['system','u1','a1','u2','a2']
snippets.explanation: Indexing, negative indices, slicing (which copies) and in-place mutation.

snippets.title: Cheap end, expensive front
snippets.code:
lst = [1, 2, 3, 4]
lst.append(5)      # O(1) amortized — end
lst.pop()          # O(1)            — end

lst.insert(0, 0)   # O(n) — shifts all later elements
lst.pop(0)         # O(n) — same reason

from collections import deque
q = deque([1, 2, 3])
q.appendleft(0)    # O(1) — use deque when both ends matter
q.popleft()        # O(1)
snippets.explanation: Reach for collections.deque when you need fast inserts/removals at the front.

snippets.title: Build a string the right way
snippets.code:
tokens = []
for t in stream():
    tokens.append(t)        # collect each streamed token (O(1) each)

text = "".join(tokens)      # one allocation, O(n)

# Avoid:
# text = ""
# for t in stream(): text = text + t   # O(n^2) — strings are immutable
snippets.explanation: Repeated string concatenation is O(n^2); collect into a list and join once.

snippets.title: Lists vs NumPy for numeric work
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

vec_a = [0.1, 0.2, 0.3]                 # Python list
vec_b = np.array([0.1, 0.2, 0.3])       # NumPy ndarray

# Lists need a manual loop or zip:
dot_py = sum(a*b for a, b in zip(vec_a, vec_a))

# NumPy is vectorized in C:
dot_np = float(vec_b @ vec_b)            # much faster at scale
print(dot_py, dot_np)
snippets.explanation: For embeddings and other numeric arrays, NumPy/Torch are far faster than Python lists.
